slides.mascot_explanation: Look at all these special things we need! The snapping frame is like a practice jacket that helps us learn. Make sure you can see everything clearly - good light helps our fingers work better!

slides.text: Set up the snapping frame on a low table with good lighting, then demonstrate slow, deliberate movements to open and close one snap.
slides.mascot_explanation: Watch carefully as your grown-up shows you how the snap works! See how slowly and gently they move their fingers? That's exactly how we'll practice together.

slides.text: Show how to align the two parts of the snap before pressing, then let your child practice opening snaps first as this is the easier movement.
slides.mascot_explanation: First we line up the snap pieces like puzzle parts, then we press! Opening snaps is easier, so that's where we start. You're going to be so good at this!

slides.prompt: Guide your child's fingers initially for closing snaps, then encourage independent practice with 2-3 snaps at a time.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now it's your turn to try! Your grown-up will help your fingers learn the right way to press. Take your time and remember - practice makes us better and better!

items: Can the child align the snap pieces before pressing?
Are they using appropriate finger pressure?
Do they show persistence when snaps don't close easily?
slides.mascot_explanation: You're doing such wonderful work with those snaps! I can see how hard you're concentrating and how much your fingers are learning. Every try makes you stronger!

slides.text: Celebrate your child's efforts and allow them to practice independently. Store the frame where they can access it for repeated practice throughout the week.
slides.mastery_check: Child can independently open at least 2 snaps and close 1 snap with minimal assistance
slides.mascot_explanation: What an amazing job you did today! Your fingers are getting so strong and smart. You can practice with this frame anytime you want - I know you'll keep getting better and better!

title: Sandpaper Letters: Tracing and Sounding Letter I
description: Children explore the letter 'i' through multisensory tracing and phonetic practice. This lesson engages touch, sight, and sound to reinforce letter recognition and phonetic awareness.
instructions: 1. Present the sandpaper letter 'i' on a colored background board
2. Demonstrate proper tracing technique using index and middle fingers
3. Trace the letter while saying the phonetic sound '/i/' clearly
4. Invite your child to trace with you several times
5. Encourage independent tracing while saying the sound
6. Practice I Spy with objects beginning with '/i/' sound (igloo, insect)
7. Let them trace the letter in a sand tray or salt tray
8. End with reviewing the sound and showing the letter shape
materials_needed: Sand tray or salt on a tray
parent_notes: The sandpaper letters provide crucial tactile input that helps children internalize letter shapes and sounds. Always use the phonetic sound '/i/' rather than the letter name 'eye'. Move slowly and deliberately when tracing, as this builds muscle memory. Some children may need many repetitions over several weeks to master each letter - this is completely normal for this age group.

slides.heading: Sandpaper Letters: Tracing and Sounding Letter I
slides.subheading: Exploring Letters Through Touch and Sound
slides.mascot_explanation: Hello, my friend! I'm Betsy Bear and I love learning about letters! Today we're going to meet a special letter that feels rough when we touch it and makes a sound like '/i/'.

items: Sandpaper letter 'i' on colored board
Small objects starting with 'i' sound
Sand tray or salt on a tray
Small basket for objects
slides.setup_instructions: Arrange materials on a table with the sandpaper letter prominently displayed and objects in the basket nearby.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at these wonderful learning tools! The rough letter feels different from smooth paper, and we have special treasures that start with the same sound. Everything is ready for our letter adventure!

slides.text: Present the sandpaper letter 'i' and demonstrate proper tracing technique using index and middle fingers while saying the phonetic sound '/i/'.
slides.mascot_explanation: Watch how your grown-up uses two fingers to trace this bumpy letter! They're saying '/i/' - that's the sound this letter makes. It's like the sound in the middle of the word 'pig'!

slides.text: Invite your child to trace with you several times, then encourage independent tracing while saying the sound '/i/'.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now it's your turn to feel the rough letter with your fingers! Trace slowly and say '/i/' just like I do. Your fingers are learning exactly how to make this letter!

slides.prompt: Practice I Spy with objects beginning with '/i/' sound, then let them trace the letter in a sand tray while continuing to practice the sound.
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's play I Spy! I spy something that starts with '/i/' - can you find it? Then you can trace the letter in the sand with your finger. It feels different but makes the same shape!

items: Does the child trace the letter correctly with two fingers?
Can they produce the '/i/' sound when shown the letter?
Do they identify objects that start with '/i/' sound?
slides.mascot_explanation: You're doing such beautiful tracing! I can see your fingers know exactly where to go. And listen to that perfect '/i/' sound you're making - just like a real reader!

slides.text: Review the sound one more time and let your child trace the letter shape in the air. Store materials for continued practice throughout the week.
slides.mastery_check: Child can trace the letter 'i' correctly and produce the '/i/' sound when prompted
slides.mascot_explanation: What a wonderful job learning about the letter that says '/i/'! You can trace it in the air anytime you want to practice. Your fingers and your brain are becoming great friends with this letter!

title: Number Rods and Numerals: Matching Quantities 1-7
description: Children practice matching number rods with their corresponding sandpaper numerals for quantities 1 through 7. This lesson reinforces the connection between concrete quantities and abstract symbols.
instructions: 1. Lay out number rods 1-7 in order on a floor mat or large table
2. Review counting each rod by touching the red and blue segments
3. Introduce sandpaper numerals 1-7 by tracing and naming each
4. Demonstrate matching numeral 1 with the shortest rod
5. Continue matching numerals 2 and 3 with their corresponding rods
6. Invite your child to match the remaining numerals 4-7
7. Mix up the numerals and let them re-match independently
8. End by counting all rods together and reviewing the numeral names
materials_needed: Number rods 1-7 (or substitute with colored blocks/units)
Large floor mat or table space
parent_notes: This activity bridges concrete and abstract thinking by connecting physical quantities with written symbols. If you don't have official number rods, you can create them with unit blocks or even draw lines with alternating colors. Focus on the one-to-one correspondence and let your child count by touching each segment. Some children may only master 1-4 initially, which is perfectly appropriate for this age.

slides.heading: Number Rods and Numerals: Matching Quantities 1-7
slides.subheading: Connecting Numbers We See With Numbers We Count
slides.mascot_explanation: Hi there! I'm Betsy Bear and I love counting things! Today we're going to match our colorful counting rods with the number symbols - it's like finding the perfect pairs!

items: Number rods 1-7 (or substitute with colored blocks/units)
Sandpaper numerals 1-7
Large floor mat or table space
Small basket for numerals
slides.setup_instructions: Lay out the number rods in order from shortest to longest on a large mat or table, with numerals nearby in a basket.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at our beautiful counting rods all lined up from tiny to long! And here are the number symbols in this special basket. We're going to be number detectives and match them up!

slides.text: Review counting each rod by touching the red and blue segments, then introduce sandpaper numerals 1-7 by tracing and naming each one.
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's count each rod by touching the pretty red and blue parts! Then we'll meet the number symbols that feel rough and bumpy when we trace them with our fingers.

slides.text: Demonstrate matching numeral 1 with the shortest rod, then continue with numerals 2 and 3 and their corresponding rods.
slides.mascot_explanation: Watch me find the perfect match! This little '1' goes with our shortest rod because they both mean 'one.' See how the '2' belongs with the rod that has exactly two parts?

slides.prompt: Invite your child to match the remaining numerals 4-7 with their rods, then mix up numerals for independent re-matching practice.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now it's your turn to be the matching expert! Take your time to find which number symbol belongs with each rod. You can count the sections to help you figure it out!

items: Can the child match at least 4 numerals correctly with their rods?
Do they count the rod segments to verify their matches?
Are they beginning to recognize numerals without extensive tracing?
slides.mascot_explanation: You are such a smart number matcher! I love watching you count the sections and find the right symbols. Your eyes and fingers are learning to be number friends!

slides.text: Count all rods together one final time and review the numeral names. Store materials for continued practice with these number relationships.
slides.mastery_check: Child can independently match at least numerals 1-5 with their corresponding number rods
slides.mascot_explanation: What amazing number work you did today! You matched the counting rods with their number symbols like a real mathematician. You can practice this matching game anytime you want!

title: Finger Painting: Spring Flower Garden Scene
description: Children use finger painting techniques to create a spring garden scene with flowers and grass. This lesson focuses on using different fingers for different textures and exploring color mixing.
instructions: 1. Set up finger painting area with aprons and protective covering
2. Provide green, yellow, red, and blue finger paints on paper plates
3. Demonstrate using fingertips for small flower centers
4. Show how to use whole fingers for flower petals
5. Practice using palm side of fingers for grass strokes
6. Encourage experimenting with color mixing for new flowers
7. Let your child create their own spring garden freely
8. Display the finished artwork and discuss the colors used
materials_needed: Finger paints in green, yellow, red, blue
parent_notes: Finger painting provides essential sensory input and creative expression opportunities. Don't worry about the final product - focus on the process and let your child explore freely. This activity develops fine motor skills, color recognition, and artistic confidence. Some children may be hesitant about getting messy initially - encourage but don't force participation.

slides.heading: Finger Painting: Spring Flower Garden Scene
slides.subheading: Creating Art With Our Hands and Imagination
slides.mascot_explanation: Hello, little artist! I'm Betsy Bear and I love making colorful art with my paws! Today we're going to paint beautiful spring flowers using our fingers - it's going to be so much fun!

items: Finger paints in green, yellow, red, blue
Large paper (11x14 or bigger)
Paper plates for paint
Aprons or old clothes
Wet wipes for cleanup
Plastic tablecloth or newspapers
slides.setup_instructions: Cover the work area with protective covering, set out paints on plates, and have your child wear an apron or old clothes.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at all these wonderful art supplies! We have bright, squishy paints and a big paper to paint on. Don't worry about getting messy - that's the best part of finger painting!

slides.text: Demonstrate using fingertips for small flower centers, then show how to use whole fingers for flower petals.
slides.mascot_explanation: Watch how I use just my fingertip to make tiny flower centers - like little dots! Then I use my whole finger to make the petals around it. Each finger can make different shapes!

slides.text: Practice using palm side of fingers for grass strokes, and encourage experimenting with color mixing for new flower colors.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now let's make grass by sliding the side of our fingers up from the bottom! And look what happens when we mix colors together - we can make brand new colors for our flowers!

slides.prompt: Let your child create their own spring garden scene freely, exploring different finger techniques and color combinations.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now it's time to make your very own spring garden! Use your fingers however feels good to you. Make big flowers, little flowers, tall grass, or anything else you imagine in your garden!

items: Is the child exploring different finger techniques?
Are they experimenting with color mixing?
Do they show engagement and enjoyment in the creative process?
slides.mascot_explanation: I love watching you create your beautiful garden! Look at all the different ways you're using your fingers and all the pretty colors you're making. You're a natural artist!

slides.text: Help your child clean up and display their finished artwork. Discuss the colors they used and what they enjoyed most about finger painting.
slides.mastery_check: Child participates willingly in finger painting and uses at least two different finger techniques
slides.mascot_explanation: What a gorgeous spring garden you created! I can see so many beautiful flowers and lovely colors. You should be very proud of your artwork - it makes me so happy to look at!

title: The Very Hungry Caterpillar: Days of the Week and Counting
description: Children listen to Eric Carle's classic story while focusing on the days of the week sequence and counting the foods eaten. This reading emphasizes patterns, sequencing, and number recognition.
instructions: 1. Show the book cover and ask your child to predict what might happen
2. Read slowly, pausing to count the foods on each page together
3. Emphasize the days of the week as you read through the story
4. Point to the holes in the pages and let your child poke fingers through
5. Discuss the caterpillar's transformation into a butterfly
6. Reread focusing on the numbers and days sequence
7. Ask your child to retell parts of the story using the pictures
8. Connect to real life: Have they seen caterpillars or butterflies?
materials_needed: Optional: calendar to point out days of the week
parent_notes: This beloved book naturally integrates counting, days of the week, and life cycle concepts. Read expressively and pause frequently for your child to participate. The repetitive text structure helps children predict and join in. Don't rush through - let them explore the unique cut-out pages and discuss the illustrations. This book can be reread many times with different learning focuses.

slides.heading: The Very Hungry Caterpillar: Days of the Week and Counting
slides.subheading: Following Our Friend Through His Week of Eating
slides.mascot_explanation: Hi, sweet friend! I'm Betsy Bear and I love story time! Today we're reading about a very hungry caterpillar who eats lots of yummy food all week long. Let's count and learn together!

items: The Very Hungry Caterpillar book
Comfortable reading area
Optional: toy caterpillar or butterfly
Optional: calendar to point out days of the week
slides.setup_instructions: Create a cozy reading spot where you can both see the book clearly and point to details in the illustrations.
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's get cozy in our special reading spot! We have our wonderful book with the hungry caterpillar, and maybe even a toy friend to join us. I can't wait to see what he eats!

slides.text: Show the book cover and ask your child to predict what might happen, then read slowly, pausing to count the foods on each page together.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at this colorful book cover! What do you think this little caterpillar will do? As we read, we'll count all the yummy foods he finds - one apple, two pears, and more!

slides.text: Emphasize the days of the week as you read and point to the holes in the pages, letting your child poke fingers through them.
slides.mascot_explanation: Listen for the days of the week - Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday! And look at these special holes in the book where the caterpillar ate through. You can put your finger through them too!

slides.prompt: Reread the story focusing on numbers and days sequence, then ask your child to retell parts using the pictures.
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's read our story again and really listen for all those numbers and days! Then you can tell me the story using all these beautiful pictures. You know this story so well now!

items: Can the child count along with the foods in the story?
Do they recognize or repeat any days of the week?
Can they describe the caterpillar's transformation into a butterfly?
slides.mascot_explanation: You're such a good listener! I heard you counting the fruits and saying some of the days. And you understand how the little caterpillar changed into a beautiful butterfly - that's amazing!

slides.text: Connect the story to real life by discussing caterpillars and butterflies they may have seen. Keep the book accessible for independent looking throughout the week.
slides.mastery_check: Child can count to at least 3 along with the story and shows understanding of the caterpillar's transformation
slides.mascot_explanation: What a wonderful story we shared! Have you ever seen a real caterpillar or butterfly outside? This book will be here whenever you want to look at it again and remember our hungry little friend!
